segments.title: Blockade as economic warfare
segments.summary: A definition of blockade leads into the Royal Navy's First World War effort to preserve British trade while denying Germany the imports that sustained its industry and food supply.
segments.body: This opening frames blockade as a state instrument for controlling sea lanes and restricting an opponent's commerce, rather than simply a dramatic action around a port. The discussion uses the British blockade of Germany to show how sustained pressure on trade could damage both industrial production and civilian morale.

evidence.note: The speaker defines close, distant, loose, and pacific blockade, then describes the Royal Navy's wartime trade mission.
He distinguishes a blockade from a siege by its purpose of stopping goods moving in or out.

segments.title: The Anaconda Plan and compounding trade pressure
segments.summary: The Anaconda Plan illustrates why a blockade can be strategically decisive even when many blockade runners still get through: each lost export also reduces the enemy's ability to finance later imports.
segments.body: Using the Confederacy's dependence on cotton exports and imported manufactures, the video traces a feedback loop in which interception cuts both income and the supplies needed to produce future exports. It also connects naval power to railways, industrial depth, and the ability to shift goods within a continental state.

evidence.note: The Anaconda Plan is linked to the Confederacy's weak east-west transport and industrial base.
A numerical example explains how a partially effective blockade creates diminishing trade returns.

segments.title: How a close blockade protects the battle fleet
segments.summary: A close blockade puts small vessels near the enemy port to watch for a sortie, while the stronger fleet waits farther offshore where weather and coastal hazards are less dangerous.
segments.body: The video explains the trade-off between immediate observation and the danger of operating near a hostile coast, using the Monitor's coastal strengths and ocean-going weaknesses as a comparison. A light inshore squadron can provide warning while preserving the main force for the decisive response.

evidence.note: The speaker discusses close blockade, inshore waters, and the Monitor's limitations at sea.
He describes a small blockading squadron warning a larger fleet held farther from the coast.

segments.title: The Continental System without sea control
segments.summary: Napoleon's Continental System shows the limits of trying to isolate a maritime trading power when Britain retains the ships, alternative markets, and smuggling routes needed to evade the restriction.
segments.body: The video contrasts the British naval blockade of France with Napoleon's attempt to prohibit European trade with Britain after Trafalgar. It argues that the policy overreached because Britain could diversify its commerce while continental enforcement relied on states and coastlines Napoleon could not fully control.

evidence.note: Napoleon's response to British blockade and the Continental System's lack of naval enforcement are described.
The discussion links smuggling, Portugal and Spain, Russia, and Britain's diversified trade to the policy's failure.

segments.title: Loose blockade and the submarine-warfare distinction
segments.summary: A loose blockade keeps warships out of hazardous coastal waters; the video then argues that unrestricted submarine warfare resembles siege warfare more than a conventional blockade.
segments.body: Operating farther from the coast reduces exposure to storms, shallow water, and sudden attacks, which is why many blockades are loose rather than close. The speaker distinguishes stopping and seizing commerce from submarine attacks that primarily destroy ships and lives, while acknowledging that the boundary is debatable.

evidence.note: Loose blockade is explained as avoiding confined and dangerous coastal waters.
The speaker contrasts seizure-based blockade with destructive unrestricted submarine warfare.

segments.title: Neutral rights, contraband, and maritime coercion
segments.summary: Blockade enforcement creates immediate disputes over neutral shipping: identifying flags, searching for contraband, impounding cargoes, and the political consequences of stopping sailors at sea.
segments.body: The discussion follows the problem from Napoleonic restrictions on neutral trade to submarine attacks on ships carrying neutrals in the world wars. It also explains why cargo inspection and impressment could turn a naval enforcement policy into a wider diplomatic crisis.

evidence.note: Neutral shipping, flags, and examples involving the Asama Maru and SS Vincent de Paul are discussed.
The speaker outlines searches for contraband, impoundment, purchases, and impressment disputes.

segments.title: Pacific blockades and the narrower tools of sanctions
segments.summary: Pacific blockades use naval pressure short of declared war, while modern sanctions and embargoes seek more selective leverage without the global disruption of a full maritime blockade.
segments.body: The video introduces pacific blockade through Greece and New Granada, emphasizing how sea access can be used to compel a political decision. It then compares the blunt pressure of blockade with targeted trade, diplomatic, military, sporting, and environmental sanctions that demand international coordination and intelligence.

evidence.note: The definitions and historical examples of pacific blockade are presented.
The speaker compares blockades with embargoes and several types of modern sanctions.

segments.title: When blockade can work in modern war
segments.summary: The closing argument weighs close against distant blockade by asking whether the target depends on seaborne trade and whether the blockading power can also address its land connections.
segments.body: A trade-dependent economy can feel blockade pressure quickly even when the cordon is imperfect, but access to alternative suppliers and land routes changes the calculation. Comparing Britain and Germany in the world wars, the video stresses that attacking shipping is not the same as controlling the sea routes that carry it.

evidence.note: The speaker relates modern blockade effects to trade dependence, supply sources, and communications.
The conclusion distinguishes blockade from siege and notes the importance of continental borders.
